title: Slow-roast shoulder of lamb
subtitle: Six hours low and slow until it falls apart
excerpt: A whole bone-in lamb shoulder roasted at 140 degrees C for six hours until the meat pulls apart with no resistance. Little effort, long time, deeply satisfying result.
sourceNotes: Standard British slow-roast shoulder technique. Bone-in shoulder for the best flavour.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze pulled meat in portions for up to three months. Reheat gently with a splash of stock.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be cooked a day ahead, refrigerated whole, and gently reheated covered in foil for 45 minutes at 150 degrees C.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking meat at a low temperature for several hours, allowing collagen to dissolve gradually into gelatin and fat to render slowly, producing tender, moist flesh.
Leaving cooked meat to stand uncovered before serving, allowing the juices to redistribute evenly through the muscle fibres rather than running onto the board.

content.text: Shoulder of lamb is the best value roasting joint. Where the leg is lean and precise in its timing, the shoulder is forgiving: the high fat and collagen content mean it almost cannot be overcooked. Six hours at 140 degrees C and it simply falls from the bone.
Remove the lamb from the fridge one hour before cooking. Heat the oven to 140 degrees C fan.
Using a sharp knife, make deep slits all over the shoulder about 2 cm apart. Push a piece of garlic and rosemary into each slit.
Rub the joint all over with olive oil and scatter the salt generously over the top and sides.
Place the shoulder fat-side up in a roasting tin. Pour the stock and red wine (if using) around the base. Cover tightly with foil.
for 5 hours covered, then remove the foil and continue for 1 further hour until the skin is golden and the meat yields completely when pressed.
Transfer to a warm board and
for 30 minutes under a tent of foil before serving.

items.symptom: Meat is still firm and not pulling apart after 6 hours
items.cause: The oven temperature was too low, or the foil seal was loose and steam escaped.
items.fix: Raise the temperature to 160 degrees C, reseal with foil, and cook for another hour. The joint needs to reach an internal temperature above 90 degrees C for collagen to convert.

items.symptom: The base of the pan has caught and burnt
items.cause: Not enough liquid in the base, or the foil was too loose.
items.fix: Add stock in the base from the start and ensure the foil is sealed tightly against the sides of the tin. Check at the 3-hour mark and top up if needed.

items.symptom: Fat on top is still soft and pale, not golden
items.cause: The uncovered hour was not long enough, or the oven was still covered.
items.fix: Remove the foil completely for the last hour. Raise the oven to 180 degrees C fan if more colour is wanted.

items.symptom: The cooking juices have reduced to nothing
items.cause: Foil was not sealed and steam escaped during the long cook.
items.fix: Deglaze the tin with 200 ml of hot stock and stir up any sticky bits for a simple sauce.

content.text: Stud with anchovy fillets alongside the garlic for a deeper savoury flavour without a fishy taste. Add whole peeled shallots to the base of the tin for the last 2 hours. A pomegranate molasses glaze brushed on in the final uncovered hour gives a Middle Eastern character.

Slow-roast lamb shoulder has roots across Mediterranean and Middle Eastern cooking. The British version settled into the Sunday lunch repertoire through the twentieth century. It is one of the few roasting joints that truly rewards neglect: the longer it cooks, within reason, the better it gets.
